You could kind of go over, well, let me also point out on the page you're looking at there, on 146, sort of the making your, well first of all, I got the word dentist every other line. I mean, I don't put up generic bullet points, but about calling what you're doing, you know, of great importance. It's not like improve your practice, it's a whole new practice model. On the next page at the bottom there, so I've cracked your code, I've cracked the code language. You can look at some of this on your own. Let me just call your attention to the most blatant of it. Here's page 149. I mentioned every sort of health related client that I've got any history with. And maybe more importantly is I don't bother mentioning non-health clients, because even if there's a really great one, the dentists only care about what is, has specific to them as is possible. They don't really care about if I'm doing FedEx or if I'm doing martial arts studios, or I'm, see, they don't care about that. They care about something that's easy for them to connect with. And so I don't waste time or space on things that don't fit that criteria, and I try and use everything that does fix that criteria. Here's on page 151. We're dealing with Barry Shamus as a speaker at a dental meeting. And if you look at number six, reduced legal risk. Doctors are pretty sensitive to this. It's sued all the time. And so the key word here is successful practices are targets. Using a proven hiring system reduces your exposure to employee lawsuits. It's not even in the promotional material I got from Barry, but he's talking about it now, because I put it in a copy and I made a fairly big deal out of it. I mean, so it's a benefit of hiring right that is important to the dentist. That might not be that important to some other small business person. If you look at page 152, I got Hartoonian. So I got to make Hartoonian as relevant to dentists as I possibly can. By the way, back to the dating thing. Not only do I switch to the Tonight Show, but he was on Regis and Kathy Lee. Well, see, that's no good to me, because it ain't Regis and Kathy Lee anymore. It's Regis and whatever her name is. They change. So I got Regis Philbin Show. So they can think now current instead of old. And all through here, I've dentistized in this case to make him relevant to this particular audience rather than using generic copy. If you look at page 155, here's a little bridge from past to present. So this is Geyer. Most of Geyer's claims to fame are from chiropractic, not from dental. That's changing quickly, but not quick enough to help me with this copy. So I've got to talk about his chiropractic facts and success, and then I got to make some kind of bridge to get him over into dentistry. Now I write all the bullets dentistry, but I got to create a bridge that gets him there. If you will look at 158 and 159, this is actually a really good example. So here's Howard Berg, who's a speed reading and memory guy. And we got him at a dental conference. So Howard's generic claim to fame, Guinness Book of World Records, et cetera. But how are we going to make him specifically relevant to dentists? So here's what I got on page 159. You'll be able to mystify and astound your patients. You'll get through continuing ed courses in a fraction of the current time. Easily recall important dental case details. Remember patients' names. So again, I'm making him, moving him from generic to specifically relevant to the dentist and why they ought to be interested in this. Now again, you're not going to get that from somebody else because all their stuff is generic. Usually a speaker, like he may have dentist testimonials, by the way, but you got to go beg for them and hunt for them and fight for them because they're not smart enough when they get in engagement with you, with insurance guys. Here's page 160 again. Here's still me making, me relevant to dentists. So for example, in one 10-year period, over 10,000 dentists and chiropractors. Well, first of all, I put dentists first. If I was sending this to chiropractors, I would have put chiropractors first and dentists second. I fuzzy the number in this case because probably 7,500 of those were chiropractors and only 2,500 of those were dentists, but that doesn't help me much. So I fuzzy it together, let them figure it out. I got my health stuff in there. I make sure I got perfect smile in there, which is the direct dental connection. And again, all the copy gets changed to specifically appeal to dentists. Here's name dropping. And in this case, I put Brian Tracy's name first, Zig, Seconds, Tom, Sturds and Jim Rohn's fourth. Why do I have Brian's first? You wouldn't know it, but Brian's been doing a lot of work in dentistry over the last couple years. So of the four of them, the person most likely to be recognized by dentists is Brian Tracy. If I was going, say, to the direct sales industry, I'd probably have Zig's name first. If I was going to real estate, I'd probably have Tommy Hopkins' name first. And so there's a lot of little micro decisions when you go to make yourself relevant to a particular audience that you really got to think about that audience. On page 163, this is Ferrores' upcoming boot camp. This is the celebrity chef guy who's going to be there. But of the ones we had to choose from, the choice was actually pretty obvious because this guy was hired and brought in by one of Ferrores' members to do a promotional event at his restaurant. Well that gives me tremendous relevance because now I don't just have a celebrity chef to talk about. I got a successful promotion that occurred in a restaurant to talk about. Plus, I get to make that restaurant owner a speaker as well. So we had four choices. This guy's the hands down winner even though he's probably the least famous of the bunch. We got Vance. This is Rory. So again, in Vance's case, I pretty much avoided talking about hardly anybody from his client list and his story list and his background except restaurants. Why bother? But the only other one I used was Steve Jobs at Apple. Now this is a, you'll notice my last sentence. In fact, nobody's been elbows deep in problem solving and breakthrough strategy development with as many restaurant operations as Mike. Well quite frankly, I have no earthly idea whether that's true or not. And I have no idea how anybody else would know whether it's true or not either. But it's a damn good sentence, isn't it? And it is supported, if you will, by the proof of the name dropping above it. But I've omitted, I haven't talked about his work with anybody that they can't relate to. There's no point in it. Because the only thing a restaurant owner thinks about is restaurants. That's where he's heads at. So I'm scrounging through all of the Vance books and all of the Vance materials to find all the restaurant crap that I can mention. And I'm omitting pretty much everything else. On page 165, this loops back a bit. But here's a, here's one of those challenge statements. So this is for the Starbucks guy. If you are not insatiably, deeply curious about the inside story of what grew Starbucks and what makes it tick, maybe you ought to find a for sale sign for your restaurant, bistro, diner, cafe, pizza, parlor, et cetera, and seek a different line of work. So that's one of those challenge statements that we talked about, right? So we've said, if this doesn't get your jets going, you're like a moron. And so that's, and that's more useful than the eight reasons why they should be on the call.
